seoTitle: Enter Opening Balances When Setting Up QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Set accurate starting balances for your bank and credit card accounts in QuickBooks Online so your books align from day one.
intro: Getting your starting numbers right when setting up QuickBooks Online prevents major reconciliation headaches down the road. This guide walks through entering an opening balance for a new account and understanding how QBO tracks it against your equity.

steps.heading: Open the Chart of Accounts
steps.body: Go to Transactions in the left sidebar and select Chart of Accounts.

steps.heading: Create a new account
steps.body: Click New in the upper-right corner, then choose your account category, such as Bank or Credit Card, and name it.

steps.heading: Enter the opening balance
steps.body: Scroll to the Balance section at the bottom of the form and enter the exact dollar value from your real-world statement.

steps.heading: Set the conversion date
steps.body: Use the As of date field to select your official accounting conversion date.

steps.heading: Save and verify
steps.body: Click Save, then check the new account's register to confirm the opening line shows correctly, and run a Trial Balance to verify everything aligns.

faq.question: What account does QuickBooks use to offset an opening balance?
faq.answer: The engine automatically books the opposite side of the entry to the Opening Balance Equity account to keep the books balanced.

faq.question: Can I fix an opening balance after I've saved it?
faq.answer: Yes, you can open the account register to review or tweak the setup figure without disturbing active bank connections.

faq.question: What date should I use for the opening balance?
faq.answer: Use your official accounting conversion date — the exact point when you started tracking this account in QuickBooks Online.

faq.question: How do I confirm my opening balances are correct?
faq.answer: Run an initial Trial Balance report after setup to verify your starting figures align across the general ledger.
